title: Magnon polaritons in a van der Waals ferromagnet coupled to a superconducting resonator
authors: Alvaro Bermejillo-Seco, Luuk J. van der Goot, Matteo Arfini, Yaroslav M. Blanter, Gary A. Steele, Herre S. J. van der Zant
abstract: Achieving magnon-photon hybridization in the microwave regime is essential for integrating magnetic excitations with superconducting circuits. While this has been extensively demonstrated in bulk magnetic systems, realizing it in two-dimensional van der Waals materials remains challenging due to their reduced magnetic volume and increased dissipation. Here, magnon-photon hybridization is observed in exfoliated flakes of the van der Waals ferromagnet Cr$_2$Ge$_2$Te$_6$, with thicknesses down to 30 nm. The resulting magnon polaritons-hybrid excitations of cavity photons and magnons-are evidenced by reproducible avoided crossings across six devices, enabled by a low-impedance superconducting resonator design. The coupling strength follows the expected square-root dependence on thickness, and extrapolation of this scaling indicates that hybridization in the monolayer limit is within reach.
summary: This paper reports the successful observation of magnon-photon hybridization in ultra-thin flakes of the van der Waals ferromagnet Cr2Ge2Te6. By using a low-impedance superconducting resonator, the authors achieved significant coupling strengths that scale with the square root of the material thickness. The results provide a pathway toward achieving hybrid light-matter states in the 2D monolayer limit.
main_problem: Achieving and characterizing magnon-photon hybridization in ultra-thin two-dimensional van der Waals ferromagnets, which is difficult due to reduced magnetic volume and increased dissipation.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated reproducible magnon-photon hybridization in Cr2Ge2Te6 flakes as thin as 30 nm, showing that the coupling strength scales with the square root of thickness and suggesting hybridization is possible in the monolayer limit.
method: Microwave transmission (S21) spectroscopy was performed using a low-impedance NbTiN superconducting resonator coupled to exfoliated CGT flakes at cryogenic temperatures.
model_or_system: The system consists of a superconducting NbTiN lumped-element resonator coupled to exfoliated flakes of the van der Waals ferromagnet Cr2Ge2Te6 (CGT). The physics is described by a coupled magnon-photon oscillator model involving Zeeman interaction.
key_observables: Avoided crossings in the frequency spectrum, resonance frequencies, linewidths (loss rates), and coupling strength (g).
important_parameters: Thicknesses down to 30 nm, coupling strength g/2pi up to 386 MHz, cavity loss kappa_c/2pi ~ 1.39 MHz, and magnon loss kappa_m/2pi ~ 650 MHz.
main_assumptions: The model for additional spectral features assumes thickness-confined standing spin-wave modes with pinned boundary conditions and neglects magnetostatic energy.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the CGT crystal structure and spin-wave absorption; Figure 3 shows the avoided crossing in a 30 nm flake; Supplemental figures show resonator frequency/linewidth evolution, device characterization, and numerical calculations of spin-wave modes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of 2D magnetism, describes the experimental platform and resonator design, presents the observation of hybridization and thickness scaling, and concludes with an analysis of higher-order spin-wave modes and the potential for monolayer hybridization.
why_interesting: This work demonstrates a platform for hybridizing magnetic excitations with superconducting circuits, which is highly relevant to the development of circuit quantum electrodynamics (cQED) and the integration of magnonics into quantum information architectures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: a Crystal structure of CGT, showing Cr spins aligned along the crystallographic c-axis due to magnetic anisotropy. b Spinwave absorption spectrum of a bulk CGT crystal measured on a 50 Ωfeedline as a function of in-plane magnetic field at 2 K. The black line is a theoretical calculation (see Section S2, Supporting Information). c Diagram of a magnon mode with frequency ωm coupled to a cavity photon mode with frequency ωc at rate g. The respective dissipation rates are κm and κc. d Schematic of the coupling mechanism. An AC current at the cavity frequency flows through the NbTiN inductor, generating an oscillating magnetic field that couples to the uniform magnon mode in the CGT...

Figure 2: a Microscope picture of a CGT flake transferred on top of the inductor wire of a low-impedance resonator (device A). The flake is 139 nm thick, as deduced from atomic force microscopy (inset). b Background-subtracted absorption spectrum of the device shown in a at 2K as a function of in-plane magnetic field showing an avoided crossing. c,d Analysis of the resonance frequency (c) and linewidth (d) as a function of the in-plane magnetic field. The data points are obtained by fitting Lorentzian line shapes to the spectra in b and Equation 3 is fitted to them. The fitted resonance frequencies of the uncoupled modes are shown as dashed lines. The extracted fit parameters are g/2π = 386...

Figure 3: a Background-subtracted absorption spectrum of 30nm flake (device B) measured at 2 K as a function of in- plane magnetic field showing an avoided crossing. The dashed line shows the fitted resonance frequencies. The inset displays an SEM image of the flake on the inductor (scale bar: 5 µm). b Overview of reported couplings between microwave cavities and van der Waals magnets as a function of flake thickness. Five devices from this work are shown in blue squares and are fitted to a square root dependence. The threshold for observing hybridisation is indicated by the blue dashed line correspond- ing to the losses measured in this work and the orange dashed line corresponding to the...

Spintronics.note: The paper is an essential direct reference as it reports the experimental realization of magnon-photon polaritons in a van der Waals ferromagnet coupled to a superconducting resonator.
